content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Adversarial Prompt Shuffling for Robust Language Model Defense",
  "problem": "Standard prompting leaves language models vulnerable to adversarial attacks and jailbreak prompts designed to elicit unsafe or unintended outputs. We lack prompting techniques that proactively disrupt adversarial prompt patterns without degrading normal task accuracy.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs are increasingly deployed in public-facing settings, adversarial misuse and jailbreaks have become urgent concerns. Current defenses often require model retraining or rule-based filtering, which are brittle and hard to maintain. Developing prompt-level defenses is a lightweight, model-agnostic way to improve robustness and can coexist with other safety strategies.",
  "method": "We propose Dynamic Adversarial Prompt Shuffling (DAPS), which intercepts user prompts and injects stochastic, semantically-preserving transformations (e.g., paraphrasing, order shuffling, synonym substitution) before passing the prompt to the LLM. The method is designed to break adversarial prompt structures that exploit specific token patterns while preserving the underlying intent for benign queries. At inference time, DAPS can adapt transformation intensity based on prompt risk assessment (via a lightweight classifier), maintaining usability for normal users. We train the prompt transformation module adversarially so that it specifically targets known attack vectors.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate DAPS on adversarial prompt datasets (e.g., AdvBench, JailbreakBench) and compare against static filtering and instruction-tuned baselines. Metrics will include attack success rate reduction, benign prompt accuracy/fluency, and user satisfaction (via human evaluation). Ablation studies will assess the impact of each prompt transformation type and adaptive intensity control."
}
